attributes.stem:
A 52-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of yellowing of her skin. She has been experiencing generalised pruritus and gradually increasing fatigue, which she attributes to menopause. She denies any abdominal discomfort. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She is a social drinker and non-smoker. There is no significant family history.

On examination, she appears well. Her vital signs are within normal limits. There is xanthelasma and generalised jaundice. Abdominal examination reveals hepatomegaly on palpation, with no guarding, rigidity, or signs of ascites.

Investigations reveal:

  • Total bilirubin: 60 μmol/L (3-20)
  • ALP: 900 U/L (30-110)
  • GGT: 500 U/L (<60)
  • AST: 70 U/L (<45)
  • ALT: 80 U/L (<55)

An abdominal ultrasound shows no obstruction in the biliary tree or any mass lesion.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations is likely to be the most useful?
attributes.explanation:
This patient's presentation is consistent with primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), a condition characterised by T-lymphocyte-mediated attack on small intralobular bile ducts. The vast majority of cases are in women, typically between 30 and 65 years of age, with initial symptoms of fatigue and pruritus often preceding jaundice. The patient's clinical features, including xanthelasma, jaundice, and hepatomegaly, along with cholestatic liver function tests, in the absence of radiological findings of biliary tree obstruction, strongly suggest PBC. Serum ALP is almost always elevated, and PBC should be suspected in cases of chronic cholestasis without a clear cause.

Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA) is the most useful investigation in this case. AMA is present in approximately 95% of patients with PBC and is a key diagnostic marker. The diagnosis of PBC is typically made based on cholestatic liver function tests, the presence of AMA, and the exclusion of biliary obstruction on imaging.

CT abdomen is unlikely to provide additional useful information in this case. An ultrasound has already been performed, showing no biliary obstruction or mass lesions.

Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) is not indicated here. The ultrasound has already ruled out biliary obstruction, and the risks associated with ERCP outweigh any potential benefits in this scenario.

Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (ANCA) is more commonly associated with primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), not PBC. PSC is typically associated with inflammatory bowel disease, and would frequently show biliary stricturing on imaging.

Chronic viral hepatitis serology is less useful in this case. The patient's liver function tests suggest a cholestatic rather than hepatocellular pattern of liver injury, and there are no reported risk factors for viral hepatitis. Additionally, the clinical presentation is more consistent with PBC than chronic viral hepatitis.

In summary, the combination of cholestatic liver function tests, typical symptoms (fatigue, pruritus, jaundice), and the absence of biliary obstruction on ultrasound make AMA the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis of PBC. If positive, it would strongly support the diagnosis and guide further management, including the initiation of ursodeoxycholic acid therapy and monitoring for disease progression and complications.
learningPoints: Anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA) is present in 95% of patients with primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) and is a key diagnostic test.
Primary biliary cholangitis typically presents with fatigue, pruritus, and cholestatic liver function tests in middle-aged women.
references: Lindor KD, Bowlus CL, Boyer J, Levy C, Mayo M. Primary Biliary Cholangitis: 2018 Practice Guidance from the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases. Hepatology. 2019;69(1).
